description: Source Reference: 2026-03-28 App Router decision follow-up. Purpose: complete the current Next.js rebuild on the App Router architecture before production cut-over, while the app is still in transition and the route files are already slim orchestrators. Scope: migrate the active Next.js routes (index, program, pt-view, rehab) plus shared shell ownership from pages/_app.js and pages/_document.js into an app/ structure. Preserve current behavior, offline/PWA support, auth flows, metadata, service worker registration, analytics, and Vercel deployment behavior. This is a controlled migration project, not a ground-up rebuild. Main file families in scope: pages/*.js active routes, pages/_app.js, pages/_document.js, route CSS modules, app/* replacements, shared components/hooks/lib touched by route migration, README.md, and testing/checklist docs if workflow expectations change.

title.description: Epic title for this App Router migration workstream

steps.title: Profile route render and interaction cost during App Router migration
steps.description: Add an explicit render-profiling lane to the App Router migration so route performance work is grounded in actual render and interaction cost, not just bundle size. Scope: collect render/interaction evidence for the active Next.js routes on realistic mobile constraints, identify where client bootstrap, rerenders, and long-list rendering dominate LCP/INP, and use those findings to guide memoization, virtualization, transition scheduling, cache-first loading, and staged bootstrap work before cut-over. Primary file scope: docs/TESTING_CHECKLISTS.md, app/* or pages/* route hosts under migration, components/ExercisePicker.js, components/HistoryPanel.js, components/HistoryList.js, components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js, and any small profiling helpers or notes added to support this analysis. Deliverable: explicit profiling findings exist for the active routes and the optimization lane can cite them directly.

steps.title: Define shared client boundaries and route-host compatibility
steps.description: Audit and adapt shared components/hooks used by migrated routes so they work cleanly under App Router client/server boundaries. Scope: identify route-host assumptions, ensure interactive surfaces are hosted under explicit client boundaries, and isolate any browser-only wrapper concerns that currently depend on Pages Router shell behavior. File scope: hooks/useAuth.js, hooks/useMessages.js, hooks/useUserContext.js, hooks/useToast.js, components/NavMenu.js, components/AuthForm.js, components/MessagesModal.js, components/Toast.js, lib/supabase.js, lib/offline-cache.js, and any migration helper wrappers introduced by the App Router shell. Deliverable: shared route hosts are App Router-safe and reusable by all migrated routes.

steps.title: Use React 18 transition and deferred state for expensive interactions
steps.description: Apply React 18 scheduling tools to the interaction paths that currently compete with urgent UI responsiveness. Scope: identify expensive state updates on the active app routes, use startTransition for non-urgent updates such as heavy exercise-selection or filter transitions, and use useDeferredValue where derived filtering/search rendering should lag typing rather than block it. Primary file scope: pages/index.js or its future app route host, components/ExercisePicker.js, pages/pt-view.js or its future app route host, pages/program.js or its future app route host, and any related hooks that coordinate selection/filter state. Deliverable: expensive route interactions are explicitly scheduled so urgent taps and typing stay responsive on phone hardware.

steps.title: Lazy-load conditionally opened modals with next/dynamic
steps.description: Use next/dynamic for modal and overlay surfaces that are currently statically imported but only rendered conditionally, so they do not all inflate the initial client bundle. Scope: identify modal surfaces on the active app routes and convert them to intentional dynamic imports where that reduces initial parse/load cost without breaking UX. Primary file scope: pages/index.js or its future app route host, pages/pt-view.js or its future app route host, pages/program.js or its future app route host, components/SessionLoggerModal.js, components/NextSetConfirmModal.js, components/SessionNotesModal.js, components/MessagesModal.js, components/ExerciseHistoryModal.js, components/DosageModal.js, and any App Router route-host replacements that own these imports. Deliverable: conditionally opened modal surfaces are lazy-loaded intentionally and verified against current behavior.

steps.title: Trim unnecessary rerenders on heavy client surfaces
steps.description: Reduce avoidable rerenders on the heaviest interactive client surfaces before cut-over. Scope: review route-host prop churn and add targeted memoization where it meaningfully reduces repeated render work without hiding stale-prop bugs. Primary file scope: components/ExercisePicker.js, pages/index.js or its future app route host, components/HistoryPanel.js, components/HistoryList.js, components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js, and any helper wrappers needed to stabilize props for memoized child components. Deliverable: heavy presentational surfaces only rerender when the props that matter actually change, with ExercisePicker treated as the first high-value target.

steps.title: Reduce route bootstrap payloads and stage data loading
steps.description: Add an explicit data-loading optimization lane to the App Router migration. Scope: audit the active app routes for oversized initial payloads, reduce first-render data requirements where possible, and stage route loading so the UI does not wait on large secondary datasets before becoming usable. Primary file scope: hooks/useIndexData.js, hooks/usePtViewData.js, hooks/useProgramPageData.js, hooks/useRehabCoverageData.js, route hosts under pages/ or future app/ equivalents, and any supporting lib helpers that shape initial route payloads. Deliverable: first-render data requirements are intentionally reduced or staged instead of pulling every secondary dataset before the route becomes usable.

steps.title: Virtualize long history and exercise lists where rendering cost is high
steps.description: Evaluate and implement list virtualization on the route surfaces where long lists materially increase render cost. Scope: identify the current high-cost list surfaces and virtualize them where profiling shows full-list rendering is a meaningful part of route slowness. Primary file scope: components/ExercisePicker.js, components/HistoryPanel.js, components/HistoryList.js, components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js, pages/index.js or its future app route host, pages/pt-view.js or its future app route host, pages/program.js or its future app route host, and any shared measurement helpers needed for virtualized list containers. Deliverable: long list surfaces only render the visible window when list length or render cost justifies it, with parity preserved for mobile interaction and scrolling behavior.

steps.title: Serve large route data from cache first and refresh in background
steps.description: Implement cache-first bootstrap where large route datasets currently block first usable paint. Scope: load available IndexedDB/offline-cache data immediately for the active heavy routes, render from that cached snapshot when present, then refresh from network in the background and reconcile visible state only when fresh data differs. Primary file scope: lib/offline-cache.js, hooks/useIndexData.js, hooks/usePtViewData.js, hooks/useProgramPageData.js, hooks/useRehabCoverageData.js, route hosts under pages/ or future app/ equivalents, and any helper code that marks cache freshness or merge behavior. Deliverable: heavy routes can paint from cache quickly instead of waiting on all network bootstrap work before showing usable UI.

steps.title: Add bundle analysis to App Router migration performance lane
steps.description: Add bundle analysis to the App Router migration so route performance decisions are based on evidence instead of guesses. Scope: enable and run bundle analysis for the active Next.js routes, identify which imports and shared modules inflate initial client bundles, and use the results to guide modal lazy loading, route splitting, and dependency cleanup before cut-over. Primary file scope: next.config.js, package.json, route hosts under pages/ or future app/ equivalents, and any small build/config helpers needed to produce and read bundle reports. Deliverable: bundle reports exist for the active app routes and the migration/optimization work uses those findings explicitly.

steps.title: Shift eligible route work off the phone with explicit server/client boundaries
steps.description: Add an explicit server/client boundary lane to the App Router migration so reduced client-side JavaScript is a tracked deliverable, not an implied side effect. Scope: review each migrated route and shared shell for which work can move into App Router Server Components or server wrappers, which work must remain client-side, and where auth/layout/data assembly should live to reduce client bundle and bootstrap cost without breaking the interactive tracker model. Primary file scope: app/layout.js and any route-level app/* files created during migration, hooks/useAuth.js, hooks/useUserContext.js, hooks/useMessages.js, route hosts for rehab, pt-view, program, and index, plus any small server-wrapper/client-island support files introduced by the migration. Deliverable: server/client boundaries are chosen intentionally and the migration explicitly reduces phone-side JavaScript where the route shape supports it.

steps.title: Tracker route: carve out any realistic server-side shell/data work without breaking interactive flow
steps.description: Evaluate the main tracker route under App Router and move only the route work that can realistically leave the phone without breaking the logging experience. Scope: keep timer/logging/offline/message interactions client-side where required, but examine whether route shell, metadata, user-context bootstrap, and stable read-heavy list/data assembly can shift into server-side ownership or thinner client islands. Primary file scope: pages/index.js or future app route files, hooks/useIndexData.js, hooks/useUserContext.js, hooks/useMessages.js, components/ExercisePicker.js, components/HistoryPanel.js, components/BottomNav.js, lib/index-history.js, lib/index-tracker-session.js, and any supporting boundary files created during migration. Deliverable: index route uses the most server-side ownership that is realistic for this app instead of defaulting to a monolithic client host.

steps.title: Rehab route: move read-heavy shell work server-side where possible
steps.description: Evaluate rehab route ownership under App Router and move eligible non-interactive shell/data work off the phone. Scope: treat rehab as the first server-boundary pilot, keeping only the accordion and other clearly interactive islands client-side while moving metadata, route shell, and any stable read-heavy data assembly into Server Components or a thin server wrapper where practical. Primary file scope: pages/rehab.js or future app/rehab route files, hooks/useRehabCoverageData.js, components/CoverageSummary.js, components/CoverageMatrix.js, lib/rehab-coverage.js, and any supporting server/client boundary files created during migration. Deliverable: rehab uses the App Router server/client split intentionally instead of remaining fully client-hosted by default.

steps.title: Program route: document and minimize unavoidable client-side editor work
steps.description: Evaluate the program editor under App Router and explicitly separate what must stay on the phone from what can move server-side. Scope: because the editor is highly interactive, preserve the mutation-heavy workspace as client code where necessary, but move route shell, metadata, and any stable bootstrap assembly into server-side ownership where practical and document the remaining unavoidable client islands. Primary file scope: pages/program.js or future app/program route files, hooks/useProgramPageData.js, hooks/useProgramWorkspaceState.js, hooks/useProgramMutationUi.js, components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js, components/ExerciseForm.js, components/ProgramDosageWorkspace.js, components/ProgramVocabEditor.js, and any supporting boundary files created during migration. Deliverable: program route does not stay fully client-hosted by inertia; the remaining phone-side work is intentional and justified.

steps.title: PT view route: move stable dashboard assembly server-side where possible
steps.description: Evaluate pt-view under App Router and move eligible non-interactive dashboard work off the phone while keeping interactive controls client-side. Scope: preserve message flows, filters, and modal behavior as client islands where needed, but move route shell, metadata, and any stable read-heavy grouping/summary assembly into Server Components or a thin server wrapper where practical. Primary file scope: pages/pt-view.js or future app/pt-view route files, hooks/usePtViewData.js, hooks/usePtViewUiState.js, components/HistoryList.js, components/PtViewNeedsAttention.js, components/PtViewSummaryStats.js, components/PtViewFiltersPanel.js, lib/pt-view.js, and any supporting boundary files created during migration. Deliverable: pt-view uses a deliberate server/client split instead of defaulting to full client hosting.

steps.title: Migrate rehab route to App Router
steps.description: Port the rehab coverage route into app/ as the pilot migration. Scope: move pages/rehab.js route ownership into App Router, replace next/head usage with App Router metadata ownership, preserve auth gating, coverage data loading, accordion behavior, and existing CSS-module styling. File scope: pages/rehab.js, pages/rehab.module.css, hooks/useRehabCoverageData.js, components/CoverageSummary.js, components/CoverageMatrix.js, components/NavMenu.js, components/AuthForm.js, lib/rehab-coverage.js, and the new app/rehab route files. Deliverable: /rehab runs from App Router with parity to current behavior while Pages Router fallback remains removable.

steps.title: Migrate pt-view route to App Router
steps.description: Port the therapist history dashboard into app/ while preserving its current interactive behavior. Scope: move pages/pt-view.js into App Router, replace next/head ownership, preserve auth gating, patient context loading, messages modal, filters, session expansion, exercise history modal, and CSS-module styling. File scope: pages/pt-view.js, pages/pt-view.module.css, hooks/usePtViewData.js, hooks/usePtViewUiState.js, hooks/useEmailNotifications.js, hooks/useMessages.js, components/MessagesModal.js, components/ExerciseHistoryModal.js, components/PatientNotes.js, components/HistoryList.js, components/PtViewNeedsAttention.js, components/PtViewSummaryStats.js, components/PtViewFiltersPanel.js, lib/pt-view.js, and the new app/pt-view route files. Deliverable: /pt-view runs from App Router with current dashboard/message behavior preserved.

steps.title: Build App Router shell and root ownership
steps.description: Create the App Router foundation that can coexist with the current Pages Router during migration. Scope: add the root app/ structure, move document/head ownership out of pages/_document.js, move global wrapper concerns out of pages/_app.js, and define the App Router home for global CSS, icons, manifest, analytics, and service-worker registration. File scope: pages/_app.js, pages/_document.js, styles/globals.css, public/icons/*, public/manifest-tracker.json, app/layout.js, app/providers or equivalent client wrapper, and any small support files needed for App Router metadata/service-worker wiring. Deliverable: App Router shell exists and can host migrated routes without breaking current Pages Router routes during the transition.

steps.title: Migrate program editor route to App Router
steps.description: Port the program editor into App Router without regressing editor, queue, or mutation workflows. Scope: move pages/program.js route ownership into app/, replace next/head ownership, preserve auth gating, offline queue behavior, workspace state, dosage modal, roles workspace, vocab editor, toasts, and CSS-module styling. File scope: pages/program.js, pages/program.module.css, hooks/useProgramPageData.js, hooks/useProgramDataSnapshot.js, hooks/useProgramWorkspaceState.js, hooks/useProgramMutationUi.js, hooks/useProgramMutationActions.js, hooks/useProgramOfflineQueue.js, hooks/useProgramVocabActions.js, components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js, components/ExerciseForm.js, components/DosageModal.js, components/ExerciseRolesWorkspace.js, components/ProgramDosageWorkspace.js, components/ProgramVocabEditor.js, components/Toast.js, lib/pt-editor.js, lib/program-offline.js, lib/program-optimistic.js, and the new app/program route files. Deliverable: /program runs from App Router with editor and offline mutation behavior preserved.

steps.title: Migrate tracker index route to App Router
steps.description: Port the main tracker route into App Router as the final and highest-risk route migration. Scope: move pages/index.js route ownership into app/, replace next/head ownership, preserve auth gating, user context, offline queue, reconnect recovery, timer/logger flow, history panel, messaging, toasts, connectivity glyph, and CSS-module styling. File scope: pages/index.js, pages/index.module.css, hooks/useIndexData.js, hooks/useIndexOfflineQueue.js, hooks/useManualLog.js, hooks/useTrackerReconnectRecovery.js, hooks/useTrackerSession.js, hooks/useSessionLogging.js, hooks/useLoggerFeedback.js, hooks/useToast.js, hooks/useMessages.js, hooks/useUserContext.js, hooks/useExerciseSortState.js, components/ExercisePicker.js, components/TimerPanel.js, components/HistoryPanel.js, components/BottomNav.js, components/NextSetConfirmModal.js, components/SessionLoggerModal.js, components/SessionNotesModal.js, components/MessagesModal.js, components/NavMenu.js, components/AuthForm.js, components/Toast.js, lib/index-history.js, lib/index-tracker-session.js, lib/tracker-performance.js, lib/session-logging.js, lib/timer-panel.js, lib/logger-timer-machine.js, lib/logger-progress-comparison.js, and the new app/ route files for the tracker home. Deliverable: the main tracker runs from App Router with parity preserved across logging, history, offline, and messaging flows.

steps.title: Run App Router parity and regression verification
steps.description: Verification lane for the App Router migration. Scope: route-by-route verification for rehab, pt-view, program, and index; confirm auth, messaging, offline/PWA, service worker registration, metadata, and key mobile behaviors still work on preview before final cut-over cleanup. File scope: docs/TESTING_CHECKLISTS.md, route files migrated under app/, shared hooks/components touched by migration, and any verification notes/screenshots/logs captured during preview checks. Deliverable: explicit verification record of what passed, what failed, and what must be fixed before final cut-over cleanup.

steps.title: Finalize App Router cut-over and retire Pages Router route surface
steps.description: After App Router routes are verified, complete the transition by removing or retiring the old Pages Router route surface and syncing docs. Scope: remove redundant pages route files once their App Router replacements are active, keep any required API or reset-password exceptions explicit, update README/docs to the new route ownership map, and ensure deployment/build config is clean after the transition. File scope: pages/index.js, pages/program.js, pages/pt-view.js, pages/rehab.js, pages/_app.js, pages/_document.js, app/* replacements, README.md, docs/README.md, docs/NEXTJS_MIGRATION_STATUS.md, docs/IMPLEMENTATION_PATTERNS.md if ownership rules change, and any config files affected by the final route source of truth. Deliverable: App Router is the active route surface for the migrated app pages and repo docs accurately reflect the new architecture.

steps.title: Apply route-level React runtime optimizations during App Router migration
steps.description: Cross-cutting runtime optimization lane for the App Router transition. Scope: apply the React and Next.js optimizations already identified for the current Next.js surface so they are handled intentionally before cut-over instead of being deferred indefinitely. This lane covers dynamic modal loading, render trimming for heavy UI surfaces, and React 18 scheduling for expensive interactions. Deliverable: the migration plan explicitly includes the identified runtime optimization work instead of treating it as optional follow-up.
